dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for a reported gunshot wound in the city of Richmond.
Hi, I'm Tina, an EMT with the rescue squad. I see you're the patient police said was shot. Let me take a look at you.
Yeah, she was shot. I was walking by when a bicycle rider up and shot her without provocation.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes for me?
Eyes are opening, but she's not actively responding. GCS eye score 4.
Can you tell me your name, please?
Can you squeeze my fingers like this? Yep, she's actively squeezing.
Airway is patent and self-maintained. No signs of obstruction.
Do you have any difficulty breathing or a sore throat?
Let me look at your chest movements. They seem symmetrical.
Respiratory rate is 12. Oxygen saturation measured at 89.
Pulse ox is low at 89. We are going to give you some oxygen now.
We're giving you oxygen through a re-breather mask.
Let me check your pulse and how you look. She has a weak radial pulse on the left arm, but the others are strong.
Let's take a look at your skin.
She's cool, clammy, and diaphoretic. Signs of shock.
I notice you have wounds on your chest and back and your left arm. You have a through-and-through injury.
Can you move your head for you? She appears to be supple with no pain.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. No signs of trauma.
Let me listen to your lungs. They are clear bilaterally.
Your stomach looks fine and soft. Any pain here?
The arm hurts the most, but I don't feel anything else.
I notice no movement in the left arm. Are you able to move your fingers?
I think I can, but it's just moving slowly.
I saw the person who shot her from a bicycle with a white shirt. I ran and called 911.
Ma'am, do you have any chronic illnesses, like asthma or allergies?
I have mild asthma, some seasonal allergies, and a history of drug use.
What medications do you regularly take?
I take Advil and aspirin.
Now, let me check your pulse. It's currently 25 beats per minute and weak.
Your breathing rate is 12 per minute.
Your blood pressure is 80 over 52.
Your blood sugar is undetectable.
Let me get your cardiac reading now. It shows artifacts and sinus bradycardia.
We're getting a dressing ready for your wounds now.
We are stabilizing your limb with a splint, giving you normal saline.
We are starting an IV in the right forearm.
We are assisting your breathing now to help your lungs.
Your pulse now is 106, faster than before; good sign.
Blood pressure is improving at 84 over 55 now.
Oxygen saturation is 95-much better.
Our sugar reading is now 131 milligrams per deciliter.
Now, it's showing sinus rhythm-just as expected.
We are considering protocols 3-15 and 3-13 for mental status and shock, as well as 4-1 for trauma.
We are performing CV-ECG 4 lead monitoring.
Your blood pressure is now 86 over 52. It's trending in the right direction.
Spo2 is at 100-well above normal and stable now.
Pulse remains at 106 for now.
Applying a new dressing now and re-stabilizing the limb with a long board.
On a scale from 0 to 10, what's the worst pain you are currently experiencing?
I would say a 5-manageable but definitely uncomfortable.
Does the pain go into your arm, chest, or any other area?
The left arm has the worst pain and doesn't feel normal at all.
Respiratory rate has dropped slightly to 20. Encouraging sign.
Blood pressure is up to 100 over 73 now-showing improvement.
Was showing artifacts and sinus arrhythmia.
Pulse remains at 104 and gradually stabilizing.
Oxygen saturation is back at 100-no respiratory compromise.
Your respiratory rate has improved to 18. Airway and breathing are stable.
Pressure now is up at 103 over 66-much better response.
Was showing artifacts, sinus arrhythmia, and now showing sinus tachycardia.
Airway is clear and still patent. No new signs of obstruction.
No signs of increased work of breathing. O2 saturation stable at 100.
Her skin remains moist and paler, but patient does improve in circulation.
Respiratory rate is down to 10. Good sign of stabilization.
We are providing assisted ventilation via BVM mask for support.
Based on protocol, we're going to transport you to VCU ED.
We're going to move you on the stretcher once we secure it down entirely.
Hospital is aware and emergency team is standing by. They expect a woman in her 40s with GSW to the chest and left arm.
I will hold her leg plate still now. You can adjust the stretcher as needed.
All five straps are on. You're secure now. Tell us if anything changes.
Your respiratory rate is now 17. It's consistent with stable breathing.
I just called VCU and they confirmed the patient will be going under the alias TRRPUMPKIN.
She is still semi-conscious but responding. We can continue our assessments.
Can I assume you have a history of other substance-related issues, like drug or substance use?
Yes, a history with nicotine use and opioid abuse. Also cocaine use in the past.
You mentioned seasonal allergies. Any new symptoms with those lately?
I see in your history you had a history of bronchitis. Any long-standing issues with that?
No, it was only once, and nothing long term.
She is still semi-conscious but responding. We can proceed with continued monitoring.
I'm with the left arm splint-ready when you are.
Any last questions before we start moving the stretcher?
Everything looks stable. Once we begin, we can transfer monitoring to hospital equipment.
The patient, Mary, is 41 years old. She was shot through the chest and arm with a through-and-through injury. She also has a history of cocaine abuse.
She has a medical history of mild asthma, chronic bronchitis, seasonal allergies, and a long history of nicotine and drug use, including opioid and cocaine abuse.
We administered an IV with normal saline to help with her hypotension and provided oxygen per mask to increase her saturation.
Vital signs improved after IV hydration and oxygen. BP was up to 103/66 and her oxygen saturation is now 100% throughout transport.
Her past substance use and asthma could complicate her recovery, so you may want to check those more thoroughly in the critical care setting.
EMS said the hospital has been notified. They are prepared for an arrival under the code name TRRPUMPKIN.
IV fluids will continue en route. The patient is currently stable with controlled breathing and positive vital signs.
All equipment is secured and patient position is stable. We should arrive in VCU ED in approximately 10 minutes.
No new signs of internal bleeding, no abdominal pain reported, and no worsening of her breathing.
We have followed protocols 3-15, 3-13, and 4-1 for mental status, shock, and general trauma management.
